The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 286, THEME, ELEVEN, MAHATMA GANDHI AND THE NATIONALIST MOVEMENT. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 286 Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be y y y y yond ond ond ond ond Fig.

The text explains that 11.1 People gather on the banks of the Sabarmati River to hear Mahatma Gandhi speak before starting out on the Salt March in 1930 In the history of nationalism a single individual is often identified with the making of a nation.

The text explains that thus, for example, we associate Garibaldi with the making of Italy, George Washington with the American War of Independence, and Ho Chi Minh with the struggle to free Vietnam from colonial rule.

The text explains that in the same manner, Mahatma Gandhi has been regarded as the ‘Father’ of the Indian nation.

The text explains that in so far as Gandhiji was the most influential and revered of all the leaders who participated in the freedom struggle, that characterisation is not misplaced.

The text explains that however, like Washington or Ho Chi-Minh, Mahatma Gandhi’s political career was shaped and constrained by the society in which he lived.

The text explains that for individuals, even great ones, are made by history even as they make history.

The text explains that this chapter analyses Gandhiji’s activities in India during the crucial period 1915-1948.

The text explains that it explores his interactions with different sections of the Indian society and the popular struggles that he inspired and led.

The text explains that it introduces the student to the different kinds of sources that historians use in reconstructing the career of a leader and of the social movements that he was associated with.

The text explains that a Leader Announces Himself In January 1915, Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi returned to his homeland after two decades of residence abroad.

The text explains that these years had been spent for the most part in South Africa, where he went as a lawyer, and in time became a leader of the Indian community in that territory.

The text explains that as the historian Chandran Devanesan has remarked, South Africa was “the making of the Mahatma”.

The text explains that it was in South Africa that Mahatma Gandhi first forged the distinctive techniques of non-violent protest known as satyagraha, first promoted harmony between religions, and first alerted upper -caste Indians to their discriminatory treatment of low castes and women.

The text explains that the India that Mahatma Gandhi came back to in 1915 was rather different from the one that he had left in 1893.

The text explains that although still a colony of the British, it was far more active in a political sense.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 288 this event were the princes and philanthropists whose donations had contributed to the founding of the BHU.

The text explains that gandhiji told the privileged invitees that “there is no salvation for India unless you strip yourself of this jewellery and hold it in trust for your countrymen in India”. “There can be no spirit of self- government about us,” he went on, “if we take away or allow others to take away from the peasants almost the whole of the results of their labour.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 290 than four hundred people were killed in what is known as the Jallianwala Bagh massacre.

The text explains that in sum, they were asked to adhere to a “renunciation of (all) voluntary association with the (British) Government”.

The text explains that students stopped going to schools and colleges run by the government.

The text explains that even those who differ from you in politics look upon you as a man of high ideals and of even saintly life.” Since Gandhiji had violated the law it was obligatory for the Bench to sentence him to six years’ imprisonment, but, said Judge Broomfield, “If the course of events in India should make it possible for the Government to reduce the period and release you, no one will be better pleased than I”.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 292 “Mahatma”.

The text explains that the act of spinning allowed Gandhiji to break the boundaries that prevailed within the traditional caste system, between mental labour and manual labour.

The text explains that source 1 Mahatma Gandhi was profoundly critical of the modern age in which machines enslaved humans and displaced labour.

The text explains that he saw the charkha as a symbol of a human society that would not glorify machines and technology.

The text explains that where most Source 2 MAHATMA GANDHI AND THE NATIONALIST MOVEMENT The miraculous and the unbelievable contd

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 294 other politicians talked down to them, Gandhiji appeared not just to look like them, but to understand them and relate to their lives.

The text explains that thus, among Gandhiji’s admirers were both poor peasants and rich industrialists, although the reasons why peasants followed Gandhiji were somewhat different from, and perhaps opposed to, the reasons of the industrialists.

The text explains that a gentleman from Gorakhpur city questioned the need to ply the charkha.

The text explains that fROM SHAHID AMIN , “GANDHI AS MAHATMA”, SUBALTERN STUDIES III, OXFORD UNIVERSITY PRESS, DELHI. Ü You have read about rumours in Chapter 10 and seen that the circulation of rumours tells us about the structure of the belief of a time: they tell us about the mind of the people who believe in the rumours and the circumstances that make this belief possible.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 296 together, which is not impossible”.

The text explains that participants would take a pledge affirming that it was “the inalienable right of the Indian people, as of any other people, to have freedom and to enjoy the fruits of their toil”, and that “if any government deprives a people of these rights and oppresses them, the people have a further right to alter it or abolish it”.

The text explains that in order to prevent the use of salt that has not paid the tax which is at times even fourteen times its value, the Government destroys the salt it cannot sell profitably.

The text explains that from various sources I hear tales of such wanton destruction of the nation’s property in all parts of India.

The text explains that 49 Ü Why was salt destroyed by the colonial government?

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 298 As with Non-cooperation, apart from the officially sanctioned nationalist campaign, there were numerous other streams of protest.

The text explains that in some towns, factory workers went on strike while lawyers boycotted British courts and students refused to attend government-run educational institutions.

The text explains that these reproduce the speeches he gave at the villages en route, in which he called upon local officials to renounce government employment and join the freedom struggle.

The text explains that i had thought that the Government might perhaps let my party come as far as Dandi, but not me certainly.

The text explains that the Government may, if it wishes, congratulate itself on acting as it has done, for it could have arrested every one of us.

The text explains that the Government deserves to be congratulated on not arresting us, even if it desisted only from fear of world opinion.

The text explains that whether the Government will tolerate that is a different question.

The text explains that 49 Source 4 Ü What does the speech tell us about how Gandhiji saw the colonial state?

The text explains that writing to the government, the District Superintendent of Police remarked, “Mr Gandhi appeared calm and collected.

The text explains that what do private letters and autobiographies tell us about an individual?

The text explains that how are these sources different from official accounts?

The text explains that read any two autobiographies of nationalist leaders.

The text explains that look at the different ways in which the authors represent their own life and times, and interpret the national movement.

The text explains that choose any event that took place during the national movement.

The text explains that try and read the letters and speeches of the leaders of the time.

The text explains that he could be a local leader from the region where you live.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III, Chapter 3: Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement
